Introduction to Probability — Study Notes

Counting, conditioning, and the famous paradoxes: intuition, math, and worked problems from a single session.

Topics: Combinatorics → Inclusion-Exclusion → Bayes → Random Walks

1. The Sampling Table: How Many Ways?

Almost all of elementary probability reduces to counting: how many ways can a thing happen, divided by how many things can happen in total. The master question is: how many ways can you pick items from items? The answer depends on two yes/no switches — does order matter, and can you reuse an item (replacement)?

Picking from Ordered Unordered
With replacement
No replacement

Running example throughout: pick from items .

① Ordered, with replacement →

Pick times in sequence. Every pick has all items available (you put each back), and order matters so . Each of the slots independently takes any of values:

Example: → AA, AB, AC, BA, BB, BC, CA, CB, CC.

Intuition. A -digit number in base . This is also the size of a sequence space — e.g. the number of length- strings over an -symbol vocabulary.

② Ordered, no replacement →

Order matters, but each item is used up once picked. The choices shrink each step: , then , then , down to :

We want only the first factors of , so we divide out the tail . Example: → AB, AC, BA, BC, CA, CB. This is the permutation count.

③ Unordered, no replacement →

Now ; we care only about which set. Start from case ② and remove the over-counting: each unordered set of items was counted times (once per ordering of those items). Divide it out:

Example: → . The binomial coefficient, "n choose k."

④ Unordered, with replacement →

Order off, but repeats allowed (AA, BB valid; AB = BA). The slick trick is stars and bars. Reframe: instead of "which items," ask how many times was each item picked? A selection becomes a list of counts summing to .

Picture identical stars (the picks) and bars (dividers splitting them into bins, one per item type):

★ ★ | |     →  (2,0,0) = AA
★ | ★ |     →  (1,1,0) = AB
| | ★ ★     →  (0,0,2) = CC

You have stars + bars = symbols. A selection is just a choice of which positions are stars:

Example: → AA, BB, CC, AB, AC, BC.

★ Key takeaway. The logic spine: start at (most permissive). Kill replacement → factors shrink → . Kill order → divide by redundant arrangements → . Bring replacement back without order → reframe as counts → . The two you'll actually use in ML: (combinations everywhere) and (size of a sequence space).

2. Vandermonde's Identity

Proved by the double-counting technique: if two expressions count the same set of objects, they are equal. Setup: a group of people — say men and women — from which we form a committee of size . Count the number of possible committees two ways.

Way 1 — the LHS. Ignore the split. Choose from the whole pool of : that's by definition.

Way 2 — the RHS. Split by how many men, , are on the committee. ranges to ; these cases don't overlap and cover everything. For a fixed : choose men from () and the remaining seats are women from (). Multiply (independent choices), then sum over all :

Both ways count the same committees, so they're equal. ∎

Intuition. No factorials were expanded — the identity falls out purely from describing one set of objects from two angles. Impossible terms self-zero: when , so the sum's bounds never need babysitting. Any time you see a sum of products of binomial coefficients, suspect someone partitioned a counting problem into cases.

3. The Inclusion-Exclusion Principle

To find the size (or probability) of a union of overlapping sets: add the singles, subtract the pairwise overlaps, add back the triples, subtract the quadruples, and so on — alternating signs until you run out of levels. It is the formula for "probability of at least one."

Two sets:

Naively counts the overlap twice, so subtract it once.

Three sets:

General form ( sets):

Intuition. The alternating signs are just bookkeeping: each correction over-corrects the next level, so you keep flipping the sign to fix the previous fix. For three sets, the triple-overlap center is added 3× by the singles and subtracted 3× by the pairs — netting zero — so it must be added back once. Add singles → subtract pairs → add triples → …

Worked Problem · deMontmort's Matching Problem

Question. Cards labeled are shuffled into random order. A "match" occurs at position if card sits in slot . What is the probability of at least one match?

Let = "card is in its correct slot." We want .

  1. Single match. Pin card ; the other shuffle freely: . There are such terms, summing to .
  2. Two matches. Pin two cards: . Number of pairs , so the pair-sum is . The cancels — this clean collapse is the magic.
  3. General . . Every level collapses to regardless of .
  4. Assemble with alternating signs.

  1. Recognize the series. Since , the expression is (truncated):

Answer: — and it converges there fast (locked by ).

★ Key takeaway. Whether you shuffle 10 cards or 10 million, the chance that at least one lands in its own slot stays . More cards = more chances to match, but each match gets rarer; the two effects almost perfectly cancel, parking the answer at forever.

4. Translating English into Set Language

Real questions come in three flavors. Spotting which one you're asked is half the battle, because each maps to a different operation.

  • "At least one occurs" → the union: . This is the inclusion-exclusion case.
  • "None occur" → complement of "at least one": .
  • "All occur" → the intersection, with a De Morgan rewrite: . ("All happen" = "not (at least one fails)".)

★ Key takeaway. The single most useful trick in probability is the complement flip: . Computing "at least one" directly is painful; "none" is usually trivial. Compute the easy side, subtract from 1.

5. Independence of Events

Events and are independent if

Knowing that happened tells you nothing about . The probability of both is just the product.

The n-event subtlety (interview trap): for many events to be jointly independent, it is not enough that every pair is independent. Every subset must factorize — every pair, every triple, all the way up. Pairwise independence does not imply full independence. Clean cases (separate dice, separate flips) satisfy full independence automatically. Notation: is often written — the comma means "and," seen everywhere in ML as joint probability .

Worked Problem · Newton–Pepys (1693)

Question. Which is most likely? (1) at least one 6 in 6 dice, (2) at least two 6's in 12 dice, (3) at least three 6's in 18 dice. Pepys thought all equal (ratios 1/6, 2/12, 3/18). Newton disagreed.

Per die: , . Dice independent → multiply freely. Use the complement flip each time.

Case 1 — at least one 6 in 6 dice:

Case 2 — at least two 6's in 12 dice (subtract "zero" and "exactly one"):

Case 3 — at least three 6's in 18 dice (subtract zero, one, two — each a binomial term):

Answer: . The first bet wins. Newton was right.

Intuition. Keeping the ratio (sixes needed)/(dice) constant does not keep the probability constant. "At least " is a tail question, and the tail tightens as you scale up — a baby law of large numbers. More dice makes the fraction of sixes more predictable, shaving the upside probability.

6. Conditional Probability

The probability of given that occurred:

Intuition. Conditional probability is about shrinking your world. The moment you learn " happened," you throw away every outcome where didn't happen. Your universe shrinks to just (the denominator). Then you ask: what fraction of this smaller world is also (the numerator , the part of surviving inside )?

Concrete picture. 100 people; 30 own a dog (); of those, 12 also own a cat (). Then : forget the 70 non-dog-owners; within the 30 dog-owners, 12 have cats. The "/100" cancels — the world shrank from 100 to 30.

★ Key takeaway. Almost all of ML is conditional. A classifier computes . A language model computes . The whole enterprise is updating beliefs given evidence — and conditional probability is its grammar.

7. Multiplication & Chain Rule

Rearrange the conditional formula by multiplying through by . You can condition in either direction, so there are two equivalent forms:

"Both happen" = "B happens, then A given B" OR "A happens, then B given A." Pick whichever direction you have numbers for.

General chain rule:

Intuition. Build the joint event one piece at a time. This is exactly how autoregressive language models factorize a sentence: . The chain rule is the math behind next-token prediction.

8. Law of Total Probability

If partition the sample space (non-overlapping, covering everything), then for any event :

Intuition. Divide and conquer for probability. may be hard directly but easy if you knew which scenario you were in. Split the world into all scenarios , compute 's probability inside each, then average — weighted by how likely each scenario is. E.g.

★ Key takeaway. This law builds — which is exactly the denominator in Bayes' theorem. That's the partnership: Bayes needs a denominator, total probability is how you compute it. A matched set.

9. Bayes' Theorem

Derivation — one line. The multiplication rule gives two expressions for : and . Set them equal and divide by . Bayes is just the multiplication rule solved for the other direction.

Why it's profound — the flip. You often know but want . You know (the test's lab-measured accuracy), but you want (do I actually have it?). Bayes flips the one you have into the one you want.

Vocabulary — the language of Bayesian ML:

  • — prior: belief before evidence.
  • — likelihood: how probable the evidence is if were true.
  • — posterior: updated belief after evidence.
  • — evidence / marginal: overall probability of the evidence (the normalizer, via total probability).

In words: posterior ∝ likelihood × prior.

Worked Problem · Aces given at least one ace

Question. A random 2-card hand from 52 cards. Given you have at least one ace, what is ?

(since "both aces" implies "at least one ace," the intersection collapses to "both").

  • Numerator: .
  • Denominator: .

Answer: .

Worked Problem · Aces given the ace of spades

Question. Same 2-card hand. Now you're told you hold the ace of spades specifically. ?

One card is pinned (ace of spades). For both to be aces, the other card must be one of the 3 remaining aces out of the 51 unknown cards:

Answer: — nearly double the previous answer.

★ Key takeaway. : naming a specific ace shrinks your world more aggressively than "some ace," ruling out the many one-ace hands that don't contain the spade. What exactly you condition on matters enormously.

Worked Problem · The Disease Test (canonical Bayes)

Question. 1% of patients have a disease. A test is 95% accurate (correct positive if sick, correct negative if healthy). You test positive. What's ?

= has disease, = tests positive. Given: , , . Want .

  1. Numerator: .
  2. Denominator via total probability (a positive can come from sick OR healthy):

  1. Combine:

Answer: only — despite a positive on a "95% accurate" test.

★ Key takeaway. Base rates dominate. The disease is rare (1%), so false positives from the huge healthy group () swamp the true positives from the tiny sick group (). When a condition is rare, even an accurate test produces mostly false positives. Ignoring the prior is one of the most expensive reasoning errors there is.

10. Conditional Independence

and are conditionally independent given if

Once you know , learning tells you nothing more about .

The crucial part: conditional independence and ordinary independence are not the same — neither implies the other. Both directions happen:

Example A — dependent normally, independent once you condition (chess opponent). You play repeated games vs an opponent of unknown strength. Without knowing strength: dependent — crushing wins in early games are evidence they're strong, lowering your odds later. Information leaks through the hidden strength. Given their true strength : conditionally independent — you already know the thing the games were hinting at, so past games tell you nothing new. Dependent unconditionally, independent given C.

Example B — independent normally, dependent once you condition (fire alarm). An alarm () has two independent possible causes: fire () or popcorn (). Normally fire and popcorn are unrelated. But given the alarm went off, if you learn it was not popcorn, the probability it was fire jumps to 1 — something set it off:

Independent unconditionally, dependent given C. This is "explaining away": two independent causes of a common effect become coupled once you observe the effect.

★ Key takeaway. Conditional independence is the foundation of Naive Bayes (assumes features independent given the label) and Bayesian networks / graphical models (whose structure is a map of conditional independences). Conditioning can both create and destroy dependence — understanding that separates people who get these models from people who memorized formulas.

11. The Monty Hall Problem

Setup. Three doors: one car, two goats. You pick a door. The host — who knows where the car is — opens a different door revealing a goat, then offers a switch. Should you switch?

Yes — switching wins of the time; staying wins only .

Intuition 1 — your first pick was probably wrong, and that never changes. Your pick was right with probability , wrong with — fixed at choice time. The host was always going to open a goat door (he knows where the car is), so his reveal gives no new info about your door. Your door stays . Since probabilities sum to 1, the entire remaining concentrates onto the one other unopened door.

Intuition 2 — the 100-door amplifier. 100 doors, one car. You pick one ( chance). The host opens 98 goats, leaving your door and one other. Your door was a wild 1-in-100 guess; the other is the lone survivor of deliberate filtering. Obviously switch — it's the car of the time.

Formal solve — Law of Total Probability. You picked Door 1. Let = "switching wins," = "car behind door ," each .

  • — car is behind your door; switching leaves it.
  • — host must open Door 3; switching lands on the car.
  • — host must open Door 2; switching lands on the car.

Answer: . Proven.

★ Key takeaway. How the data was generated matters. The host's choice is constrained by knowledge — he never reveals the car. That constraint injects information into the other door. If he opened a door randomly and happened to show a goat, it genuinely would be 50/50. Same observation, different probability, because the process differs. You cannot compute without knowing the mechanism that produced your observation.

12. Simpson's Paradox

Setup. Two doctors, two operations. Hibbert beats Nick at each operation — yet Nick has the better overall success rate. Both are true at once.

Hibbert

heart band-aid
success 70 10
failure 20 0

Nick

heart band-aid
success 2 81
failure 8 9

Per operation — Hibbert wins both:

  • Heart: Hibbert vs Nick .
  • Band-aid: Hibbert vs Nick .

Overall — Nick wins:

  • Hibbert: .
  • Nick: .

Hibbert is better at every operation, yet worse overall. The paradox is real, not arithmetic error.

Why — the confounder. Hibbert did mostly heart surgeries (90 of 100) — hard, low success. Nick did mostly band-aids (90 of 100) — trivial, high success. Nick's gaudy overall number reflects easy cases, not skill. Operation type is a confounder — a hidden variable affecting both which doctor you get and the success rate. Aggregating secretly compares "mostly-hard Hibbert" vs "mostly-easy Nick."

In conditional-probability language. With =success, =treated by Nick, =heart surgery:

Within each operation Nick loses; aggregated, the inequality flips. is the confounder.

★ Key takeaway. A constant production threat. In A/B tests, a variant can win overall but lose in every segment if traffic was split unevenly. In fairness, a model can look unbiased in aggregate while discriminating in every subgroup. Defenses: always segment; hunt for confounders before trusting any comparison; remember the fix is causal, not statistical — which number is "right" depends on the question (choosing a heart surgeon? use the per-operation number). You cannot resolve it with more math, only by understanding how the data arose.

★ Key takeaway (shared with Monty Hall). The numbers alone never tell you the answer — you must understand the process that generated the data. "Think about the data-generating process" is the single most valuable reflex in probabilistic reasoning, and exactly what interviewers probe with these two problems.

13. Random Walk & Gambler's Ruin

A 1D random walk is a sequence of steps on the number line: move +1 with probability or −1 with probability . Repeated coin flips pushing left or right.

Gambler's Ruin framing. Players A and B bet \p1 from B), loses with . A starts with \(n-i), what's ?

Setting up — first-step analysis. Let i)pi+1qi-1$):

A difference equation (discrete cousin of a differential equation). Boundaries: (A ruined), (A won).

Solving it:

  1. Guess . Substitute and divide by : , i.e. .
  2. Quadratic formula: . Since , the discriminant is a perfect square, giving roots and .
  3. Two distinct roots → general solution (for ).
  4. Apply boundaries. . .

Result — biased game ():

Result — fair game (): Here gives ; an L'Hôpital limit of as yields:

Your chance of winning everything is just your share of the money. 30% of the chips → 30% chance.

The devastating table — , equal start ():

Total stake
20 0.40
100 0.12
200 0.02

A microscopic edge (49% vs 51%), starting with exactly half the money — yet at \$200 total, A wins only 2% of the time.

★ Key takeaway. A small edge, repeated forever, is destiny. Over many steps the drift dominates the noise — the bias never sleeps. Counterintuitively, playing longer makes the disadvantaged player do worse: more rounds = more chances for the bias to express. This is why "I'll keep playing until I'm up" is a guaranteed losing strategy against any house edge. Used in: risk-of-ruin in trading (fees/slippage = the edge), insurance reserves, startup runway vs burn, genetic drift (allele fixation = ), and randomized-algorithm analysis.

14. Walks in 1D, 2D, 3D & Beyond

Drop the absorbing boundaries and ask of an infinite walk: if you wander forever, are you guaranteed to return to the start? The answer flips with dimension — Pólya's theorem.

Dimension Return probability Behavior
1D (line) 1 Recurrent — certain to return (infinitely often)
2D (grid) 1 Recurrent — still certain, surprisingly
3D (space) Transient — ~66% chance of never returning
keeps dropping Transient, increasingly so

Intuition. The one-liner (Pólya / Kakutani): "A drunk man will find his way home, but a drunk bird may get lost forever." The 2D/3D break is the deep fact — low dimensions trap you in recurrence, high dimensions set you free into transience. The boundary sits exactly between 2 and 3.

Real-world cases by dimension:

  • 1D: stock price / account equity, diffusion in a tube, queue length, inventory level, single-allele genetic drift, startup cash runway.
  • 2D: animal foraging, robot-vacuum random coverage (recurrence is why random bouncing eventually covers a floor), molecule diffusing on a membrane or catalyst surface, disease spread on a map.
  • 3D: gas molecule / perfume dispersing (transience = why smells don't reconcentrate), pollutant in air or ocean, neutron transport in a reactor, light scattering through fog or tissue (medical imaging, volumetric graphics), polymer-chain configuration.
  • High-d () — most ML-relevant: SGD is a biased random walk through million-dim parameter space (gradient = drift, minibatch noise = kicks) — high-d transience helps escape saddle points. MCMC sampling explores high-d posteriors; random-walk Metropolis degrades with dimension, motivating HMC/NUTS. The curse of dimensionality — sparse, vast spaces where points rarely revisit — is the same geometry.

How the math changes. Bounded 1D gives the clean quadratic/difference-equation solve above. For return probability you instead sum over all ways to return and ask whether that series converges (transient) or diverges (recurrent): it converges for , diverges for . Bounded higher-d versions analyze hitting probabilities of absorbing surfaces and typically need PDE methods (the continuous limit is the heat/diffusion equation).

★ Key takeaway. The reflex: whenever something explores a space randomly — money, molecules, robots, gradients, samplers — ask "what dimension is this effectively in?" That alone predicts whether it gets trapped and revisits (low-d) or escapes and disperses (high-d), and that qualitative split governs an enormous range of real systems.

End of session notes. Introduction to Probability: Counting · Inclusion-Exclusion · Conditioning · Bayes · Random Walks.
